result.markdown:
# R is Friend

::: callout
## Learning Objectives

+ Identify three of the foundational data types in R
+ Explain similarities and differences between different object types
+ Use indexing to extract elements from different object types
+ Use functions to calculate summary statistics
+ Use logical values and indexing to calculate summary statistics
:::

## Data Types

In the last class, you learned how to create simple objects (e.g., `x <- 1221`, 
`name <- "jane"`), but we never talked about the difference between a value 
like `1221` and `"jane"`. These two values have different **data types**---one
is a **number** and one is a **character** (really, a set of characters). These 
are two of what are called the ["atomic" data types in R](https://cran.r-project.org/doc/manuals/r-release/R-lang.html#Vectors). Let's 
dig into these data types more!

### Numeric Values

You've seen a lot of numbers in your day, so it probably wouldn't surprise you
to know that there are different types of numbers. There are numbers **with**
decimals and there are numbers **without** decimals. Technically, R separates
these two types of numbers into their own data types---**doubles** and
**integers**.

#### Double (Real) Values

If a number has a decimal value (e.g., `26.2`), then R will store the number 
as a double (real) numeric. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
room <- 26.2
typeof(room)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "double"
```


:::
:::


::: {.callout-tip}
# `typeof()`

The `typeof()` function tells you the storage type that R is using internally to
hold the data. You can think of this function asking the question, 
"How is this data physically stored in memory?". 
:::

#### Integer Values

Numbers that **do not** have decimals are called `integer`s. If you want a number
to be stored as an `integer`, you need to tell R to do so. Otherwise, your
number will still be stored as a `double`.

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

Defaults to `double`:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
room <- 1221
typeof(room)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "double"
```


:::
:::


:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
Adding an `L` to the end creates an `integer`:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
room <- 1221L
typeof(room)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "integer"
```


:::
:::

:::
:::

::: {.callout-opinion}

You might wonder why `L` is used to create an `integer`. Wouldn't an `I` make 
more sense?

Because R is written mostly in C (another programming language), it likely
inherits some naming conventions from C. In C, `L` is used to denote a (long)
integer. Moreover, it would be confusing to use an `I` to indicate an `integer`
since you use a `"i"` suffix to create a complex number. 

:::

::: {.callout-tip}
# Other ways to create an `integer`

Adding an `L` at the end of a number is one way to make an integer, but this
really only works when we are creating our objects by hand. More frequently, 
we will be given a vector of numbers which we may want to transform into 
integer values. The `as.integer()` function can help us do just that!


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
room <- 1410
room <- as.integer(room)
typeof(room)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "integer"
```


:::
:::

:::

<!-- We could choose to highlight how `class()` returns the same value (numeric) for -->
<!-- both doubles and integers.  -->

<!-- class() tells you the high-level type that R uses to decide how to behave with -->
<!-- the object. Think of it as asking: "What role does this object play?"  -->

<!-- Imagine a chef's knife: -->
<!-- typeof() → "It's made of stainless steel" (the raw material) -->
<!-- class() → "It's a kitchen tool" (the role it plays) -->

### Character Values

A `character` value is somewhat the opposite of a number in that it contains 
qualitative information. In R, the `character` data type represents objects 
that can have any number of characters (e.g., `"a"`, `"ab"`, `"abc"`). A 
`character` data type can be identified by the use of quotation marks (`""`)
surrounding the values. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
first_name <- "lilly"
typeof(first_name)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "character"
```


:::
:::


::: {.callout-tip}
# Converting a number into a `character`

Converting between data types is something we will frequently find ourselves 
doing. Time related variables (e.g., `year`, `month`) are variables that are 
typically read in as numeric but we are often interested in using them as a
character. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
year <- 1988
as.character(year)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "1988"
```


:::
:::


Notice the number now has quotations around it!
:::

### Logical Values

The last data type is one we haven't seen before. Logicals have two possible 
values---`TRUE` and `FALSE`. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
passed <- TRUE
typeof(passed)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "logical"
```


:::
:::


::: {.callout-tip}
# Relationship with Numeric Values

Logical values have binary numerical representations.

::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}
A `TRUE` is represented by a `1`.

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
as.numeric(TRUE)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 1
```


:::
:::


:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}
A `FALSE` is represented by a `0`. 

::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
as.numeric(FALSE)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 0
```


:::
:::

:::
:::
:::

### Other Data Types

So far, we've covered real (double), integer, character, and logical data types.
These form all but two of the atomic data types. The only two that remain are
**complex** and **raw**. As you might expect from its name, the complex data
type is used to store complex numbers (e.g., `4 + 3i`). The raw data type is 
possibly the least used data type, whose purpose is to store data at the byte
level.

In STAT 1810 and 3820 we will **only** work with real, integer, character, and
logical data types!

## Object Types
<!-- Intro to vectors, lists, matrices, dataframes, tibbles.  -->
<!-- Building up objects -->
<!-- Small object (from last week) is a scalar value -->
<!-- Bigger object is a vector (multiple values) -->
<!-- What if we have multiple vectors? -->
<!-- To store them in a matrix, they all need to be the same data type. -->
<!-- If they are different data types, then we need a list.  -->
<!-- What if the vectors are all the same length? -->
<!-- Then we can use a data frame (or a tibble)! -->

Now that we've learned about the different types of data values, let's learn
about the different ways data could be stored. 

<!-- Give basic info / examples. -->
<!-- How to create each type of structure. -->

### Vectors

You encountered a vector in your last lab assignment! Vectors are created using
the `c()` function (which stands for concatenate). Vectors have a few important
qualities:

- They have **one** dimension, their length. 
- Their entries must all have the **same** data type. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
names <- c("Jim", "Weun", "Pat", "Pran", "Phupha", "Tian")
length(names)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] 6
```


:::
:::


In the last lab, you added a character to a vector of numbers. Even though the
objects had different data types, R didn't output an error message. Indeed, it
concatenated the objects together into one vector. But what data type was the
resulting vector?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
c(67, 13, 1989, "hello")
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[1] "67"    "13"    "1989"  "hello"
```


:::
:::


It made all the numbers into characters! This is called **data type coercion**, 
where R changes / coerces the data type of elements of the vector so they are
all the same data type. 

::: {.callout-check-in}

For each of the examples below, identify what data type do you believe the
vector will have.

1. `c(67, 13, 1989, TRUE)`
2. `c("hello", "goodbye", TRUE)`
3. `c("TRUE", FALSE, TRUE, "FALSE")`
:::

### Multiple Vectors

When working with data, we will find ourselves with *multiple* vectors---one for
each variable that was recorded. When assembling multiple vectors into an object
the key question is whether the vectors all have the **same** data type or 
**different** data types.

#### Do all of the vectors have the same data type?

Vectors that all have the **same** data type can be made into a matrix. A matrix
has **two** dimensions---rows and columns. We can create a matrix of values from
a vector by specifying the number of rows (`nrow`) or the number of columns 
(`ncol`). 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
names <- c("Jim", "Weun", "Pat", "Pran", "Phupha", "Tian")
```
:::


::: columns
::: {.column width="48%"}

Specifying the number of rows (`nrow`):


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
matrix(names, nrow = 3)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1]   [,2]    
[1,] "Jim"  "Pran"  
[2,] "Weun" "Phupha"
[3,] "Pat"  "Tian"  
```


:::
:::

:::

::: {.column width="4%"}
:::

::: {.column width="48%"}

Specifying the number of columns (`ncol`):


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
matrix(names, ncol = 3)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1]   [,2]   [,3]    
[1,] "Jim"  "Pat"  "Phupha"
[2,] "Weun" "Pran" "Tian"  
```


:::
:::

:::
:::

The vector above has length 6, which is divisible by 3. What do
you think will happen if the length of the vector isn't divisible by the number
of rows?


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
new_names <- c("Jim", "Weun", "Pat", "Pran", "Phupha", "Black", "Sean")

matrix(new_names, ncol = 3)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stderr}

```
Warning in matrix(new_names, ncol = 3): data length [7] is not a sub-multiple
or multiple of the number of rows [3]
```


:::

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1]   [,2]     [,3]  
[1,] "Jim"  "Pran"   "Sean"
[2,] "Weun" "Phupha" "Jim" 
[3,] "Pat"  "Black"  "Weun"
```


:::
:::

First, R gives a warning message telling us that the length of the vector 
(7) is not a multiple of the number of rows we input (`3`). 
Then, it **recycles** the first value of the vector (Jim) to fill
the spot where there wasn't a value. 

This can produce misleading data, so we should **always** make sure the length
of our vector is a multiple of the dimension of the matrix (e.g., rows, columns)
we are trying to make. 

::: {.callout-check-in}
4. Draw out how this matrix will look:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", 
           "Bad Buddy", 
           "Moonlight Chicken", 
           "Not Me")

matrix(shows, ncol = 2)
```
:::

:::

::: {.callout-opinion}
You might have noticed that the formatting of `shows` is a bit different from
what we used before. Why is that?

One of the formatting guidelines for writing code is that your code should not
be more than 80 characters long. This line of code is 82 characters long:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", "Bad Buddy", "Moonlight Chicken", "Not Me")
```
:::


Since it is longer than 80 characters, we need to break up the code. Meaning, we
need to use a new line (return) somewhere in our code. There are few different 
ways we could have done this:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", "Bad Buddy", "Moonlight Chicken", 
           "Not Me")

shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", "Bad Buddy", 
           "Moonlight Chicken", "Not Me")
```
:::


But, to us, these make it harder to see the length of the vector. So, that is 
why we chose to use a new line for every entry of the vector. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
shows <- c("Tale of a Thousand Stars", 
           "Bad Buddy", 
           "Moonlight Chicken", 
           "Not Me")
```
:::


:::

#### Do the vectors have different data types?

What if we wanted to combine vectors that have **different** data types? Since 
a matrix requires all the entries to be the same data type, we will need to find
a new solution! 

A **list** is an object that allows entries to be different data types. Suppose
for each show we have its release date. The name of the show is a `character` 
and the release date of the show is `numeric`. We can use a list to combine 
these two vectors into one object (`top_bl`). 




::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
release_years <- c(2021, 2021, 2023, 2022)

top_bl <- list(shows, release_years)
top_bl
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[[1]]
[1] "Tale of a Thousand Stars" "Bad Buddy"               
[3] "Moonlight Chicken"        "Not Me"                  

[[2]]
[1] 2021 2021 2023 2022
```


:::
:::

The output of a list is a bit unexpected, so let's break it down. The `[[]]` 
represents the number of the entry. So, `[[1]]` represents the `shows` and
`[[2]]` represents the `release_years`. The `[]` represents the number of the
item *within* each entry. So, `"Tale of a Thousand Stars"` is the first (`[1]`)
item of the first entry (`[[1]]`). 

We find it easier to think of lists as cubby bins. Each `[[]]` represents a bin
and the `[]` represent the items in the bin. Within a bin the objects are the 
**same** data type, but each bin can have different types of objects. 

![A `list` analogy with a cubby bin. Each cubby has different types of items (e.g., puzzles, dolls, legos) but within a cubby the items are all the same.](images/02-list-cubbies.jpg){fig-alt="A picture of a wood cubby bin with six different cubby slots. Each cubby is a different color (red, yellow, blue, green, purple, orange) and each cubby contains different types of objects (legos, books, cars, paints, dolls). However, within a cubby bin the items are all the same. Meaning, the bin of dolls does not also contain legos." width=50%}

We could have modified our method for creating the list by giving each entry 
a name. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
top_bl <- list(show_name = shows, release_year = release_years)
top_bl
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$show_name
[1] "Tale of a Thousand Stars" "Bad Buddy"               
[3] "Moonlight Chicken"        "Not Me"                  

$release_year
[1] 2021 2021 2023 2022
```


:::
:::


Notice now there are `$` symbols instead of `[[]]` symbols. The `$` in
front of `show_name` and `release_year` represents the **name** of that entry. 
We technically call this a *named list* since every entry has a name.


![A `list` analogy with a cubby bin. Similar to before, each cubby has different types of items (e.g., puzzles, dolls, legos) but within a cubby the items are all the same. However, now the cubbies all have names identifying the contents of the cubby.](images/02-lists-labeled-cubbies.png){fig-alt="Another picture of a cubby bin with nine different cubby slots. The difference with this cubby bin is that each bin has a label indicating its contents (legos, dolls, trucks, balls, art supplies)." width=50%}


#### Are the vectors all the same length?
<!-- dataframes or tibbles! -->

Technically, a list can have **both** entries with different data types
**and** entries with different lengths. Both `show_name` and `release_year` 
have 4 items. However, there is more than one character in 
each show. We can add these characters to a list in the same way as before:


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
characters <- c("Jim", "Weun", "Pat", "Pran", "Phupha", "Black", "Sean")

top_bl <- list(show_name = shows, 
               release_year = release_years, 
               characters = characters)
top_bl
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$show_name
[1] "Tale of a Thousand Stars" "Bad Buddy"               
[3] "Moonlight Chicken"        "Not Me"                  

$release_year
[1] 2021 2021 2023 2022

$characters
[1] "Jim"    "Weun"   "Pat"    "Pran"   "Phupha" "Black"  "Sean"  
```


:::
:::


The list has no problem having entries with different lengths! But, if your
entries all happen to have the **same** length, then you can use a special type
of list---a dataframe. 


::: {.cell}

```{.r .cell-code}
data.frame(show_name = shows,
           release_year = release_years)
```

::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
                 show_name release_year
1 Tale of a Thousand Stars         2021
2                Bad Buddy         2021
3        Moonlight Chicken         2023
4                   Not Me         2022
```


:::
:::


While a dataframe is a list, the output looks **very** different. Each variable
(`show_name`, `release_year`) is slotted into a column. The numbers on the side
(`1`, `2`, `3`, `4`) represent the entry number.


::: {.callout-check-in}

::: {.cell}

:::


Using the output below, identify what type of object you believe was created.

5. What type of object?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
[[1]]
[1] "Malus domestica"     "Citrus sinensis"     "Citrus limon"       
[4] "Citrus aurantifolia" "Mangifera indica"    "Prunus persica"     

[[2]]
[1]  3  5 12 12  5  3
```


:::
:::


6. What type of object?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
     [,1] [,2]
[1,]    3   12
[2,]    5    5
[3,]   12    3
```


:::
:::


7. What type of object?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
  fruits               trees
1  apple     Malus domestica
2 orange     Citrus sinensis
3  lemon        Citrus limon
4   lime Citrus aurantifolia
5  mango    Mangifera indica
6  peach      Prunus persica
```


:::
:::


8. What type of object?


::: {.cell}
::: {.cell-output .cell-output-stdout}

```
$tree
[1] "Malus domestica"     "Citrus sinensis"     "Citrus limon"       
[4] "Citrus aurantifolia" "Mangifera indica"    "Prunus persica"     

$harvest_window
[1]  3  5 12 12  5  3
```


:::
:::

:::
